justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are substantively populated with multi-sentence analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups with specific impact per group (far exceeding the 3 minimum). 3 escalations raised for missing context (business motivation, scope/segments, date ambiguity). Contractual SLA risk is explicitly identified in BR-5, business_impact.risk, and escalation #2. confidence.level is 45, well below the 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions listed. Competitive positioning is mentioned in the user impact section ('competitors offering 2-hour windows will appear superior').

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder analysis with 7 groups including non-obvious ones like API consumers, analytics, and executive leadership
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — appropriately low given the missing contract and motivation information
Escalations are specific and actionable, with concrete options and recommendations for each
Identified the metric gaming risk — that widening windows could mask operational inefficiency rather than improve it
BR-5 correctly identifies contractual SLA customers as an invariant that cannot be unilaterally changed
Edge cases include API breaking change risk and timezone handling — both commonly missed
Rejection reasons provide clear rationale for blocking the change as proposed
Competitive positioning explicitly addressed: 'competitors offering 2-hour windows will appear superior'

trials.justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are substantively populated with multi-sentence analysis covering both positive and negative dimensions. stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups (B2C customers, B2B clients, customer support, operations/dispatch, product/marketing, sales, data/analytics) each with specific impact descriptions. 4 escalations raised for missing context and ambiguity. Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in BR-6, business_impact.risk, and a dedicated escalation. confidence.level is 52, well within the ≤80 threshold, with detailed assumptions listed. Competitive positioning explicitly addressed in the risk section ('major carriers are moving toward MORE precision'). The output exceeds rubric requirements across all pass criteria.

observations.text: Exceptional stakeholder analysis with 7 distinct groups, far exceeding the minimum 3 required
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the missing contract information, with clear reasoning for the uncertainty
4 escalations covering business rationale, B2B contracts, same-day delivery, and premium tiers — all with concrete options and recommendations
Competitive risk explicitly called out: 'major carriers and e-commerce platforms are moving toward MORE precision, not less'
Financial analysis balances both sides: inflated on-time metrics and reduced refunds vs. premium churn and B2B renegotiation risk
BR-6 proactively establishes that B2B contractual SLAs must be preserved as an invariant, with testable assertion
Edge cases include premium tier exclusion, mixed-granularity reporting, and same-day delivery — all highly relevant
First escalation challenges the premise itself, asking whether better ETA predictions would be preferable to hiding imprecision

trials.justification: All 6 pass criteria met: (1) All 4 business_impact sub-fields contain substantive, multi-sentence analysis covering both positive and negative dimensions. (2) stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups (end consumers, enterprise/B2B partners, customer support, drivers/couriers, operations/dispatch, product/marketing, finance/claims) each with specific impact descriptions. (3) Three formal escalations raised for missing information: tier scope ambiguity, API vs UI scope, and rollout strategy. (4) Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in BR-2 (marked as invariant), in business_impact.risk, and in the first escalation. (5) confidence.level is 55, well within the ≤80 threshold, with 5 listed assumptions and clearly separated high/low confidence areas. (6) Competitive positioning explicitly addressed in both business_impact.user ('Industry trend is toward MORE precision — Amazon, UPS, FedEx') and business_impact.risk ('Competitive disadvantage — competitors offering 2-hour windows will appear superior').

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder analysis with 7 distinct groups, far exceeding the minimum of 3, each with specific and differentiated impact descriptions
Confidence level of 55 is well-calibrated — correctly reflects the missing contract information and underspecified scope
Strong escalation quality — the tier-scope ambiguity escalation directly addresses the contractual SLA risk the rubric requires, and includes concrete options with recommendations
Business rules BR-2 and BR-5 correctly identify invariant contractual and premium-tier constraints, showing deep domain reasoning beyond surface-level impact analysis
Financial analysis captures both directions: revenue erosion on premium tiers AND potential savings from fewer missed-ETA refund claims
Edge cases are excellent — same-day delivery, midnight boundary, multi-package orders, and recipient availability coordination all demonstrate real-world domain expertise
Operational analysis correctly balances the benefit (driver flexibility, route optimization) against the cost (internal systems still need timestamps, complacency risk)

The specific rubric-expected escalation phrasing about existing customer contracts with time-precise SLA commitments is present but distributed across BR-2, risk section, and escalation #1 rather than as a single pointed question — still clearly meets the criterion but could be sharper as a direct ask

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to change delivery ETA display from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a fundamental change to the delivery promise made to customers — the core contract between a courier/logistics company and its end users.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking & Customer Communication — the bounded context responsible for surfacing delivery commitments to customers, partners, and int
